did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed SkinAI, a web-based deep learning system using the Xception convolutional neural network to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven diagnostic categories. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset with preprocessing techniques like image augmentation and SMOTE to address class imbalance, achieving an accuracy of 85% for multi-class skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing 'SkinAI,' a web-based deep learning application using the Xception CNN on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific sections for 'Proposed System,' 'Implementation,' and 'Results' where new performance metrics (85% accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores) are reported. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICICIS), it contains original results rather than just summarizing prior literature.

title: SkinAI – A Deep Learning Based Web Application for Multi-Class Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Integration of Computational Intelligent System (ICICIS)
brief_summary: This study presents SkinAI, a web-based deep learning application designed to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven diagnostic categories for multi-class skin cancer detection. The system utilizes an Xception convolutional neural network trained on the HAM10000 dataset, employing image augmentation and SMOTE to address class imbalance. The model was implemented using Flask and TailwindCSS to provide real-time predictions and downloadable PDF reports for users. Evaluation on a test set of 1,400 images demonstrated an overall accuracy of 85%, with precision and recall values of 85.96% and 84.86%, respectively. The model achieved particularly high F1-scores for melanoma (0.98) and actinic keratoses (0.99), highlighting its effectiveness in identifying high-risk lesions. The authors conclude that SkinAI serves as a reliable, accessible decision-support tool for early-stage skin cancer detection in areas with limited dermatological care.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using an Xception convolutional neural network to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven diagnostic categories. The model predicts discrete class labels and associated probabilities. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (85%), precision (85.96%), recall (84.86%), and F1-scores for each class.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A well-known public dataset consisting of more than 10,000 high-resolution dermoscopic images of different pigmented skin lesions, created by Tschandl and colleagues. It is a standard tool for training and evaluating skin cancer classifiers.

use.training: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset. Preprocessing included scaling images to 72x72 or 82x82 pixels, data augmentation (flipping, rotation, zooming, brightness changes), and SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to address class imbalance.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on a test set of 1,400 images from the HAM10000 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (85%), precision (85.96%), recall (84.86%), and F1-scores.

HAM10000.experiment: SkinAI (Xception CNN with SMOTE and Image Augmentation)
HAM10000.notes: The model achieved particularly high F1-scores for melanoma (0.98) and actinic keratoses (0.99). Comparatively lower scores were observed for dermatofibroma (DF) and vascular lesions (VASC). The paper references Table 1 for full evaluation metrics, Figure 2 for correct vs incorrect predictions, and Figure 6 for the confusion matrix, but does not provide the specific per-class precision/recall/F1 values for all seven classes in the text.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained weights from ImageNet were utilized to enable transfer learning.

architecture_details.selection_rationale: Selected for its deep connection and effective feature reuse, making it ideal for medical image processing tasks requiring high sensitivity and accuracy.

preprocessing: Normalization of pixel values to [0, 1] range
Data augmentation (flipping, rotation, zooming, brightness changes)
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) at the feature vector level
training_details.classes: Seven diagnostic categories (Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, and Vascular Lesions)

classification_or_output_head.task: Multi-class classification into seven diagnostic categories
classification_or_output_head.output: Class prediction along with related probabilities

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNNs with support vector machines and decision trees
preprocessing_techniques: Scaling images to 72x72 pixels (Methodology) or 82x82 pixels (Implementation)
Data augmentation including flipping, rotation, zooming, and brightness changes
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) at the feature vector level to address class imbalance
Normalization of pixel values to the [0, 1] range
Flattening image arrays into numerical arrays
Use of OpenCV and NumPy libraries for preprocessing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Geometric and photometric transformations applied to dermoscopic images during training to address class imbalance and enhance model resilience. Specific techniques included flipping, rotation, zooming, and brightness changes.
Applied at the feature vector level to artificially extend minority classes and balance the dataset. Implemented using the imbalanced-learn package after flattening image arrays and before training.

conclusions: SkinAI, a web-based deep learning application using the Xception convolutional neural network, effectively classifies dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven diagnostic categories with an overall accuracy of 85% on a test set of 1,400 images.

The model demonstrated high reliability in differentiating between benign and malignant lesions, achieving particularly high F1-scores for melanoma (0.98) and actinic keratoses (0.99), which highlights its utility in identifying high-risk lesions.
Performance was comparatively lower for dermatofibroma (DF) and vascular lesions (VASC), indicating a need for further improvement through dataset enrichment or advanced data augmentation techniques.

The study validates the effectiveness of the Xception architecture combined with preprocessing pipelines including image augmentation and SMOTE for addressing class imbalance in medical image classification.
SkinAI serves as a reliable, accessible decision-support tool for early-stage skin cancer detection, particularly beneficial in areas with limited access to dermatological care.

Future work includes validating the system in real-world clinical settings through collaborations with certified dermatologists, expanding the dataset to include higher-resolution photos and underrepresented skin tones to reduce bias, and integrating multi-modal data such as patient history and symptoms to improve diagnostic accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a known public dataset with potential demographic biases (e.g., underrepresentation of darker skin tones), limiting the generalizability of the model to diverse global populations.

The paper acknowledges that performance was comparatively lower for Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC), indicating specific class weaknesses that require further dataset enrichment or advanced augmentation.

The model was trained on a test set of 1,400 images from the HAM10000 dataset; however, the paper does not report results from an independent external validation cohort, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific distribution of HAM10000.

SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) was applied at the feature vector level after flattening image arrays, a preprocessing choice that may introduce synthetic artifacts not present in natural dermoscopic images.

The system is currently a proof-of-concept web application; the authors explicitly state that future work is needed to validate the system in real-world clinical settings with certified dermatologists before clinical deployment.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported metrics (85% accuracy, 0.98 F1 for melanoma), making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the performance claims.

The input size inconsistency between the Methodology section (72x72 pixels) and the Implementation section (82x82 pixels) suggests a lack of rigorous documentation or reproducibility details regarding the exact preprocessing pipeline.

The application generates downloadable PDF reports and provides medical information fetched from a pre-linked JSON file, which raises concerns about the clinical accuracy and liability of the static medical advice provided to users without professional oversight.
